title: DA 2PLSM measurements with genetically encoded probes
author: Enrico Zampese

steps_raw.instruction: Brain slices expressing GES are obtained according to protocol and held at room temperature in a chamber containing aCSF continuously bubbled with 95% O2/5% CO2 blood gas mixture until the moment of the experiment.
Turn on 2PLSM working station, including heated stage, and the computer.

Start running aCSF through the peristaltic pump, into the microscope chamber. Check also that the chamber outlet is removing solution from the chamber at the same rate, collecting it into a waste solution collector. A vacuum-based outlet is also recommended because this can help prevent overflow, if available.
Temperature probe should be inserted in the solution in the chamber. As the system is turned on, the temperature in the microscope chamber should reach 32-33C.
Turn on imaging software.

Once the temperature in the microscope chamber is approaching the desired one, transfer one slice from the holding chamber into the imaging chamber. Adjust its position accordingly and gently place a slice holder on top of it, making sure that it doesn’t cover any region of interest.

With the eyepieces and using the LED as a light source, first verify with a low magnification objective the correct expression of the GES and adjust the stage position so that the region of interest can be easily imaged.
Switch to 60x immersion lens and visualize the surface of the slice, turn off LED and switch to the 2PLSM settings.

With the imaging software, preliminary adjust power and image acquisition settings (it is recommended starting from lower settings and increasing laser power and/or gain if needed) and start imaging in “live” mode. The 2P excitation wavelength adopted for the probes mentioned above, all based on GFP, is 920nm. Other kinds of probe might yield better results with different excitation wavelengths.

Identify a region to image from, optimize imaging settings including zoom, field of view, resolution, dwell time, frame rate. For experiments imaging dLight1.3b our preferred settings are: 64x64pixels image size, zoom 8, 8 µs dwell time, whole-field spiral scan so that the frame rate with these settings is ~40 frames per second.

Laser power and PMTs gain are adjusted so that the fluorescence at baseline is bright but far from reaching saturation of the signal. In our conditions, signal saturation is experienced above 4095 fluorescence units; the baseline fluorescence for the object of interested is normally adjusted to average at around 1000 units. Due to whole-field imaging, an acquisition should be taken with the laser shutter closed to measure background fluorescence. This is usually around 100 units.

It is recommended to wait at least 10-15 mins after placing the slice in the chamber and lowering the 60x objective before starting any experiment. This should give sufficient time to the slice to stabilize and equilibrate properly with the working temperature of the chamber. Not waiting a sufficient time might result in changes in focus/movement and instable fluorescent baseline.
2PLSM imaging experiments with GES can be performed either as time-lapse experiments or as continuous acquisitions, depending on the time-scale of the phenomenon under observation.

If desired, for a semi-quantitative approach, it is recommended to perform an in situ calibration of the dynamic range of each probe for each slice examined. This is obtained by estimating the maximum fluorescence levels for each slice.

For slow pharmacological effects, time-lapse acquisitions are preferred. In this case, a series of frames are acquired at regular intervals. Each series of frames is then averaged and represents one time-point in the time lapse. Standard acquisition settings are 60 frames over ~15 sec, acquired every 10 minutes.
For faster effects (e.g. acute stimulation), continuous acquisitions are preferred. In PrairieView this is performed as a Brightness Over Time acquisition.

To obtain the maximum fluorescence (Fmax), aCSF containing a saturating concentration of the neurotransmitter/neuromodulator (e.g. 100 µM acetylcholine chloride for GRABACh3.0) is washed in. The response is fast with the GES reaching saturation within approximately 5 minutes. BOT acquisition is performed until a stable maximum level is obtained.
Slices are discarded and the imaging chamber is cleaned after Fmax measurements.
Discard slices and waste solutions according to institutional protocols.

Carefully wash tubing, microscope chamber, slice holder, 60X objective and any part that comes in contact with the experimental solution. Water followed by 10% Ethanol is generally recommended. Rinse with water for a second time after 10% ethanol.
Turn off all the equipment according to instructions.
Export data and proceed with image analysis.

Once the region of interest is appropriately positioned on the stage, the iris diaphragm should be restricted so that a sub-region (within the area displaying expression of the GES) can be chosen precisely.

Place the tip of the tungsten bipolar electrode onto the slice. The tip should be located on the boarder of small circle of light generated by restricting the condensers iris. The location and intensity of the stimulation depend on the experimental design but should remain the same for all the experiments.
